response: create_tasks with: task_name='Call the dentist', defer_date='2026-03-16', due_date='2026-03-20'. Defer date hides the task until Monday March 16; due date sets the deadline for the Friday appointment.

1) create_projects(name='Kitchen Renovation', project_type='sequential'). 2) create_tasks with 5 items in order: [{task_name:'Get quotes', project_id:<new_proj_id>}, {task_name:'Pick contractor', project_id:<new_proj_id>}, {task_name:'Sign contract', project_id:<new_proj_id>}, {task_name:'Demolition', project_id:<new_proj_id>}, {task_name:'Installation', project_id:<new_proj_id>}]. In a sequential project, task order = dependencies, so only the first incomplete task is available.
get_tasks(available_only=True). This returns only tasks that are currently actionable — excludes blocked, deferred, completed, and tasks in inactive containers.

update_tasks([{id:'task-001', flagged:true}, {id:'task-002', flagged:true}, {id:'task-003', flagged:true}]). Flagging marks them for today's focus. Then use get_tasks(flagged_only=True) to see the focused list.

update_tasks([{id:'task-123', task_name:'Buy groceries'}, {id:'task-456', flagged:true}, {id:'task-789', flagged:true}]). A single batch call handles mixed field updates — each item specifies only the fields it needs changed.
update_tasks([{id:'task-100', project_id:'proj-200'}]). Setting project_id moves the task from inbox (or any project) to the specified project.
update_projects([{id:'proj-888', status:'dropped'}]). Dropping preserves the project and all its data in OmniFocus but marks it as abandoned. Do NOT use delete_projects — that permanently removes it.
reorder_task(task_id='task-B', before_task_id='task-A'). This places task-B before task-A. In a sequential project, order determines dependencies, so task-B will now be available first.

Two calls: 1) create_tasks([{task_name:'Review PR', tags:['Computer', 'Work']}]). Tags on create uses the tags parameter with a list of existing tag names. 2) update_tasks([{id:'task-555', add_tags:['Urgent']}]). For an existing task, use add_tags to append without replacing existing tags.

update_tasks([{id:'task-300', due_date:''}]). Passing an empty string clears the date. Note: if the task's project has a due date, the task will still show that inherited due date in get_tasks results — but the task's own direct due date will be removed.

create_tasks([{task_name:'Buy nails', parent_task_id:'task-400'}]). Use parent_task_id to create a subtask. Do NOT also pass project_id — parent_task_id and project_id are mutually exclusive. The task inherits its project from the parent.
get_tasks(tag_filter=['Errands', 'Weekend']). The default tag_filter_mode is 'and', so this returns only tasks that have BOTH tags.

Multiple calls to gather context: 1) get_tasks(flagged_only=True) — see what's already flagged for focus. 2) get_tasks(overdue=True) — find overdue items needing attention. 3) get_tasks(available_only=True) — see all actionable tasks. 4) get_tasks(inbox_only=True) — check inbox for unprocessed items. Then synthesize recommendations from these results.

1) create_projects([{name:'Website Redesign', folder_path:'Work', project_type:'sequential', review_interval_weeks:2}]). 2) create_tasks with 6 items in order, all with project_id=<new_proj_id> and tags=['Web Team']: [{task_name:'Wireframes', ...}, {task_name:'Design mockups', ...}, {task_name:'Frontend build', ...}, {task_name:'Backend API', ...}, {task_name:'QA testing', ...}, {task_name:'Launch', ...}]. Sequential project ensures order = dependencies.

get_projects(). Check the returned nextReviewDate field for each project. Projects where nextReviewDate is in the past (before today) are overdue for review. There is no direct 'overdue_review' filter parameter, so compare dates client-side.

update_projects([{id:'proj-777', status:'done'}]). This marks the project as completed. Do NOT use status='dropped' — that means abandoned. 'done' is the correct status for a successfully finished project.

set_focus does not support tasks — it only works with projects and folders. You cannot focus on a single task. Alternatives: 1) Flag the task with update_tasks([{id:'task-999', flagged:true}]) and use the Flagged perspective. 2) If the task belongs to a project, use set_focus(item_ids='<project_id>', item_types='project') to focus on that project, then filter within it.

1) get_tasks(inbox_only=True) to retrieve all inbox tasks and their IDs. 2) update_tasks([{id:'<id1>', completed:true}, {id:'<id2>', completed:true}, ...]) for each task returned. Note: if any are recurring, completed=True will create the next occurrence (this is correct behavior via 'mark complete').

No tool call needed — this is an interpretation question. Nothing is wrong. This task is an action group (a task with subtasks). The server instructions state: 'Action Groups: Task with subtasks. Parent shows blocked: true while subtasks are active — this is normal, not an error. Do not try to unblock it.' The parent remains blocked until its 3 subtasks are completed. Since sequential=true, only the first subtask is available.

No tool call needed — this is correct behavior. The server instructions explain: 'The next field is true for the first available action in a sequential project/action group; in parallel projects, all incomplete tasks have next: true.' So in parallel projects, all tasks are equally 'next'; in sequential projects, only the first incomplete task is next. Both behaviors are working as designed.

No tool call needed — but the user's observation contradicts the documented behavior. The server instructions say: 'Dates returned by get_tasks are always effective (inherited). A task with no direct due date WILL show its project's due date in the dueDate field.' So the tasks SHOULD show April 15 in their dueDate field. If they show empty, this may be a bug or the project's due date may not actually be set. Suggest: get_projects(project_id='proj-q2') to confirm the project's due date is actually set.

No tool call needed — explanation only. They are likely different project types. Check the projectType field from get_projects. 'Home Repairs' (tasks in any order) is likely project_type='parallel'. 'Errands' (grab-bag, unrelated) is likely project_type='single_actions' — a single actions list has no completion goal and cannot auto-complete. Both show sequential=false (deprecated field), but projectType distinguishes them. Use get_projects(project_id=<id>) for each to check the projectType field.

No tool call needed — the server instructions explicitly guarantee: 'completed=True on recurring task creates next occurrence.' Using update_tasks([{id:<task_id>, completed:true}]) calls 'mark complete' under the hood, which creates the next occurrence. The recurrence is preserved.

create_tasks([{task_name:'Write blog post', planned_date:'2026-03-18', due_date:'2026-03-20'}]). Planned date is a scheduling signal — it indicates when you plan to do it, but does NOT defer/hide the task. The task remains available before Wednesday. Due date sets the Friday deadline.

create_tasks([{task_name:'Prepare presentation', defer_date:'2026-03-16', planned_date:'2026-03-18', due_date:'2026-03-20'}]). Three dates: defer_date hides it until Monday (can't start earlier), planned_date signals the Wednesday work plan, due_date sets the Friday deadline.
update_tasks([{id:'task-600', planned_date:''}]). Empty string clears the planned date.

No tool call needed. The server instructions say: 'repeatSummary — human-readable recurrence; always use this for display, don't parse RRULE.' The repeatSummary field says 'Every week on Mon, Wed, Fri' — that's the answer. The task repeats three times per week on Monday, Wednesday, and Friday on a fixed schedule (repetitionMethod='fixed' means dates don't shift based on completion).
update_tasks([{id:'task-810', recurrence:'FREQ=WEEKLY;INTERVAL=2'}]). This changes the recurrence from every week to every 2 weeks using an RRULE string.

No tool call needed. The server instructions explain: repetitionMethod 'due_after_completion' means the due date shifts relative to completion. So the next occurrence will be due one week from today (the date you completed it), NOT next Monday on a fixed schedule. 'fixed' would keep the original Monday schedule; 'due_after_completion' resets from the actual completion date.
update_tasks([{id:'task-900', recurrence:''}]). Setting recurrence to empty string clears the recurrence rule, stopping it from repeating.

update_tasks([{id:'task-820', recurrence:'FREQ=DAILY;INTERVAL=1', repetition_method:'due_after_completion'}]). FREQ=DAILY sets daily repetition, and repetition_method='due_after_completion' means the next due date is calculated from actual completion date rather than a fixed calendar schedule.

update_tasks([{id:'task-830', recurrence:'FREQ=DAILY;INTERVAL=1', repetition_method:'fixed'}]). Sets a daily recurrence with 'fixed' repetition method, meaning it repeats on the same fixed daily schedule regardless of when completed.

update_tags([{id:'tag-050', status:'dropped'}]). The server instructions say: 'Dropped (tag hidden from picker; does NOT affect task availability).' So dropping hides it from the tag picker while tasks with this tag remain available and actionable. This is different from 'on_hold', which would exclude tasks from the Available perspective.

update_tags([{id:'tag-052', status:'active'}]). Change 'Waiting' from 'on hold' to 'active'. On Hold status causes tasks with that tag to be excluded from the Available perspective. Setting it to Active makes tasks with this tag available again. No change needed for 'Archive' (tag-053) — it's already dropped and stays hidden as requested.

create_projects([{name:'Someday/Maybe', project_type:'single_actions'}]). The server instructions say: 'single_actions (no completion goal, cannot auto-complete).' This is exactly the project type for a grab-bag list of ideas with no overall completion goal.
update_folders([{id:'folder-999', status:'dropped'}]). Setting status to 'dropped' hides the folder from normal views without deleting it. This archives it while preserving all its contents.
